sources.name: Wikipedia — Mega Man X3
sources.notes: Zero playability, Ride Armors, Doppler Stage mechanics.

sources.name: TASVideos — SNES / Mega Man X3
sources.notes: Physics documentation. Page availability not confirmed.

notes: Mega Man X3 continues the same engine as X1/X2. Core physics constants assumed identical.
MAJOR ADDITION: Zero is PLAYABLE for the first time. Saber melee combat vs X's buster ranged.
MAJOR ADDITION: Ride Armors — large mech suits X or Zero can pilot.
Zero's gameplay adds a melee-focused variant within the same levels.
Doppler Stages: 4 bonus stages after the 8 Mavericks, creating a 12-stage structure.
If Zero is destroyed, he cannot be used for the rest of that playthrough — risk management around Zero's use.
Walk speed, dash, jump, gravity assumed same as X1/X2. VERIFY from ROM.

x_physics._note: Same engine as MMX and X2 — all base physics values inherited.

air_dash.description: Same as X2's Crystal Chip — dash in the air.
air_dash.notes: Air dash may be available from game start in X3 or require a specific upgrade. VERIFY availability.

wall_kick.description: Same as X1/X2. Slide along wall, press jump to launch in opposite direction.

zero_gameplay._note: THE KEY ADDITION. Zero is playable for segments. Completely different combat style from X.

access.description: Zero can be called in to take over from X in certain stages. Press select-equivalent to switch.
access.risk: If Zero is destroyed (health depleted to zero), he cannot be used again that playthrough.
notes: The permadeath-per-run risk of using Zero creates genuine stakes around his deployment.
Expert players may deliberately avoid using Zero early to preserve him for harder sections.
Casual players may lose Zero early and never experience his capabilities.

z_saber.description: Zero's beam saber. Short range, high damage per hit. Completely different from X's buster.
notes: Melee only — Zero has no charge shot, no buster equivalents.
Must close range on every enemy — higher risk, higher damage.
The Z-Saber's arc covers front and slightly above/below Zero.

zero_dash.description: Zero can dash similar to X — same button, same dash speed.
zero_dash.notes: Zero uses the same movement engine as X — dash, wall kick all available.

zero_vs_x.comparison: Zero: melee range, high damage, no charge, higher risk. X: ranged buster, charge shot, special weapons, lower risk.
zero_vs_x.notes: The two characters present different risk profiles. Zero's melee demands positioning skill; X's ranged play allows more safety.

ride_armors._note: Large mech suits that dramatically change movement and combat.
ride_armors.description: Giant mech suits found in certain stages. X or Zero can pilot them.

capabilities.punch: Powerful melee attack — one-shots most regular enemies
capabilities.jump: Large jump — can reach areas normal X/Zero cannot
capabilities.walk: Slower ground movement than X/Zero on foot

durability.description: Ride Armors have their own health bar. When depleted, the mech is destroyed.
durability.notes: The pilot ejects when the armor is destroyed — X/Zero survives but loses the armor.

notes: Ride Armors create a power fantasy section — briefly overpowered and destroying everything.
The transition back to on-foot play after losing the armor creates a vulnerability moment.
Some stages have Ride Armor-specific routes (certain terrain only navigable in armor).

doppler_stages.description: 4 extra stages (Doppler Stages) unlock after defeating all 8 Mavericks.
notes: Extends the stage count beyond the standard 8-boss structure.
Dr. Doppler is the main antagonist — the Doppler Stages are his lair.
These stages use remixed Maverick abilities and tougher enemy configurations.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Zero's playability with permadeath-per-run stakes is a brilliant risk-reward design. The player is CONSTANTLY deciding: 'Is this section worth using Zero for?' Using Zero is powerful but permanently reduces your options. The capability is valuable precisely because it's consumable.

The X vs Zero gameplay split (ranged vs melee) within the same levels creates two different movement grammars for the same space. X players approach enemies at buster range; Zero players approach to saber range. The level's spatial design must accommodate both distances.

Ride Armors create 'power fantasy interludes' — brief periods of massive power before returning to the core game. The contrast (invincible in armor → vulnerable on foot) makes the standard gameplay feel more intense after the armor moment. Power fantasy as pacing tool.

Mega Man X3 is the end of the SNES MMX trilogy. By the third iteration, the engine is deeply familiar and the additions (Zero, Ride Armors) are accumulated on a stable foundation. This is what mature engine development looks like: not replacement but careful accretion.

The Doppler Stage extension changes player expectations: after 8 bosses, the traditional 'Wily stages begin' phase arrives but with more stages than expected. The villain is Dr. Doppler, not Dr. Wily — but the structural pattern (boss rush → lair stages) is the series template.
